t1.title: [ES] What is Backend Engineering?
t1.what: [ES] Backend engineering is the discipline of building server-side systems that power applications — APIs, databases, authentication, business logic, and infrastructure.
t1.why: [ES] Every product needs a reliable, scalable backend. Frontend is the face; backend is the brain and backbone. Without it, no data persists, no logic executes, no system scales.
t1.when: [ES] Whenever you build anything beyond a static page: user accounts, data storage, real-time features, payments, AI integrations, or multi-service architectures.
t1.how: [ES] A client sends an HTTP request → the server routes it → middleware processes auth/validation → a handler executes business logic → the database is queried → a response is returned.
t1.ecosystem: [ES] Node.js, Python (Django/Flask/FastAPI), Go, Java (Spring Boot), Rust, PostgreSQL, Redis, RabbitMQ, Kafka, Docker, Kubernetes, AWS/GCP/Azure.
t1.realWorld: [ES] Netflix processes 2+ billion API requests daily. Stripe's backend handles payment processing across 46 countries. Uber's backend orchestrates 25M+ rides daily.
advantages: [ES] Full control over data and business logic
[ES] Security boundaries at the server level
t1.interviewTip: [ES] When asked 'What does a backend engineer do?', go beyond 'write APIs.' Talk about system reliability, data integrity, scalability patterns, and operational excellence.
t1.resumeBullet: [ES] Designed and implemented RESTful backend services handling 10K+ requests/sec with <50ms p99 latency using Node.js and PostgreSQL.

lcet1.title: [ES] What is Low-Code Engineering?
lcet1.what: [ES] Low-code engineering is the discipline of building software applications using visual development platforms that minimize the need for manual hand-coding. It combines pre-built components, visual models, and automated workflows.
lcet1.why: [ES] It significantly reduces time-to-market, addresses the shortage of specialized skills, and allows for rapid prototyping and deployment of enterprise-grade applications.
lcet1.when: [ES] Ideal for business process automation, internal tools, customer portals, and when speed of delivery is more critical than low-level system control.
lcet1.how: [ES] Developers use drag-and-drop interfaces to design UIs, define data models, and orchestrate logic via visual flows, adding custom code only for complex integrations or unique logic.
lcet1.ecosystem: [ES] OutSystems, Mendix, Microsoft Power Platform, Appian, ServiceNow, Salesforce Lightning, APIs, connectors, and cloud services.
lcet1.realWorld: [ES] A global logistics firm used a low-code platform to build a comprehensive freight tracking system in just 4 weeks, a project previously estimated at 6 months.
lcet1.interviewTip: [ES] Emphasize how low-code is a strategic choice for business agility, not a replacement for 'real' coding. Highlight your ability to extend low-code with custom logic when needed.
lcet1.resumeBullet: [ES] Accelerated application delivery by 75% using low-code platforms for a fleet management system, integrating with legacy SOAP APIs via custom connectors.

t2.title: [ES] HTTP, REST & API Design
t2.what: [ES] REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications using HTTP methods to perform CRUD operations.
t2.why: [ES] REST is the lingua franca of web APIs. Understanding it deeply separates junior developers from engineers who can design systems that last.
t2.when: [ES] Whenever you need machine-to-machine or client-server communication. REST is ideal for stateless, cacheable, resource-oriented interactions.
t2.how: [ES] Resources are identified by URIs. HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) map to operations. Status codes communicate outcomes.
t2.ecosystem: [ES] OpenAPI/Swagger, Postman, cURL, Express.js, FastAPI, Spring Boot, API Gateway (AWS/GCP), rate limiters, API versioning strategies.
t2.realWorld: [ES] GitHub's REST API serves millions of developers daily. Stripe's API is considered the gold standard.
disadvantages: [ES] Can become chatty for complex operations
t2.interviewTip: [ES] Know the difference between PUT and PATCH. Understand idempotency. Be able to design a clean API for any domain in under 5 minutes.
t2.resumeBullet: [ES] Architected RESTful API layer with OpenAPI spec, versioning strategy, and rate limiting, reducing integration time by 60%.

t3.title: [ES] SQL & Relational Databases
t3.what: [ES] Relational databases organize data into tables with defined schemas, relationships (via foreign keys), and use SQL for querying. They guarantee ACID properties.
t3.why: [ES] Most business data is inherently relational. Understanding SQL deeply is non-negotiable for any backend engineer.
t3.when: [ES] When you need strong consistency, complex queries with joins, transactions, or well-structured data with clear relationships.
t3.how: [ES] Data is stored in rows within tables. Indexes speed up reads. Query planners optimize execution. Transactions ensure atomicity.
t3.ecosystem: [ES] PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, SQL Server. ORMs: Prisma, TypeORM, Sequelize, SQLAlchemy, Hibernate.
t3.realWorld: [ES] Instagram runs on PostgreSQL and handles billions of rows. Banks rely on ACID-compliant databases for transactions.
disadvantages: [ES] Can be slower for simple key-value lookups
t3.interviewTip: [ES] Be ready to write complex SQL queries on the spot. Know the difference between INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, and CROSS JOIN. Understand indexing trade-offs.
t3.resumeBullet: [ES] Optimized PostgreSQL queries reducing p95 latency from 800ms to 45ms through strategic indexing and query plan analysis.

hceFront101.title: [ES] Frontend Engineering 101
hceFront101.desc: [ES] Master the core of the web platform.
hceFront101.targetAudience: [ES] Engineers wanting to master the fundamental building blocks of the web before adopting heavy frameworks. Essential for anyone wanting to build performant, accessible UIs natively.

outcomes.o1: [ES] Write semantic, accessible HTML5 layouts that rank high in SEO and support screen readers inherently
outcomes.o2: [ES] Style complex responsive grids and flexboxes using modern CSS3 natively without UI component libraries
outcomes.o3: [ES] Manipulate the Document Object Model (DOM) directly with high-performance Vanilla JavaScript and Event Delegation
outcomes.o4: [ES] Fetch and display dynamic data from external REST APIs using Promises/Async-Await without crashing the main thread
outcomes.o5: [ES] Configure modern module bundlers (Vite) and development servers for a rapid development lifecycle

m1.title: [ES] The Web Platform Architecture
m1.desc: [ES] How browsers parse text streams of HTML, CSS, and JS to paint pixels on a GPU.

outcomes.o1: [ES] Understand the Critical Rendering Path (DOM + CSSOM = Render Tree)
outcomes.o2: [ES] Write Semantic HTML for SEO and A11y out-of-the-box

t1.title: [ES] Semantic HTML & The Critical Rendering Path
t1.what: [ES] Semantic HTML uses specific tags (like <article>, <nav>, <main>) instead of generic <div> tags to give structural meaning to web content.
t1.why: [ES] A page built purely with <div> tags requires massive amounts of JavaScript to make accessible to screen readers, and is ranked poorly by search engines.
t1.when: [ES] This is the absolute first layer of any web architecture.
t1.ecosystem: [ES] Blink, WebKit, Gecko engines. Web Vitals: LCP, FID, CLS.
t1.realWorld: [ES] Google and major news sites use semantic structure for accessibility and SEO.
t1.interviewTip: [ES] Explain the sequence: URL -> HTML -> DOM -> CSSOM -> Render Tree -> Layout -> Paint.
t1.resumeBullet: [ES] Optimized Critical Rendering Path for high-traffic pages, reducing LCP by 40%.

v2.title: [ES] Semantic HTML: What, Why, and How
v2.author: [ES] Fireship

s1.title: [ES] The Blank White Screen of Death
s1.description: [ES] A page remains white for 8 seconds on 3G connections due to a massive JS block in the <head>.
s1.analysis: [ES] The DOM Parser is blocked by synchronous JS execution.

t1.name: [ES] The `defer` attribute
t1.justification: [ES] Allows background download and deferred execution.

tradeOffs.t1: [ES] Functionality delay of a few milliseconds

community.aiSummary: [ES] Community focuses on the trade-offs between 'defer' and 'async'.

t2.title: [ES] Scalable CSS Architecture
t2.what: [ES] Scalable CSS Architecture (BEM, SMACSS) manages large-scale styling without the 'Cascade' becoming a nightmare.
t2.why: [ES] Global CSS leads to side-effects where changing one style breaks 50 other pages.
t2.when: [ES] Crucial on Day 1 of any project.
t2.ecosystem: [ES] PostCSS, Tailwind, BEM, CSS Modules, Styled Components.
t2.realWorld: [ES] Design Systems at Slack and Spotify are built on Design Tokens for instant global updates.

useCases.u2: [ES] Reducing CSS Bloat in enterprise apps

disadvantages.d1: [ES] BEM class names can become long.

t2.interviewTip: [ES] Use BEM or CSS Modules to keep selectors flat and avoid specificity wars.
t2.resumeBullet: [ES] Architected a modular CSS system using BEM, resulting in 70% decrease in dead CSS.

s1.title: [ES] The !important War
s1.description: [ES] Modal background won't change due to global resets.
s1.analysis: [ES] High specificity scores and global selectors cause the cascade to fail.

t1.name: [ES] CSS Modules
t1.justification: [ES] Automatically scopes classes to unique hashes.

s1.insights: [ES] Scope your styles or the cascade will scope you.

p1.title: [ES] BEM vs Utility Sandbox
p1.desc: [ES] Compare BEM and Tailwind for a button component.

community.aiSummary: [ES] CSS methodologies are essential for modern scalable applications.

m2.title: [ES] Advanced Layout & Performance
m2.desc: [ES] Mastering the layout engines and optimization patterns.

t1.title: [ES] Advanced Layouts: CSS Grid vs Flexbox Architecture
t1.what: [ES] Grid and Flexbox are the primary layout systems in modern front-end engineering.
t1.why: [ES] They allow fluid layouts without JavaScript intervention.
t1.when: [ES] Flex for components, Grid for page structure.
t1.ecosystem: [ES] Box Model, Flexbox, Grid, Responsive Design.
t1.realWorld: [ES] Netflix home screen uses CSS Grid for massive scale.

disadvantages.d1: [ES] Grid has a steep learning curve.

t1.interviewTip: [ES] Flex is for content, Grid is for layouts.
t1.resumeBullet: [ES] Engineered a responsive enterprise dashboard using CSS Grid.

s1.title: [ES] The Fractional Layout
s1.description: [ES] Static calculations fail to account for gutters properly.
s1.analysis: [ES] Brittle CSS breaks when small styles change.

t1.name: [ES] CSS Grid 'fr' units
t1.justification: [ES] Browser handles borders and gaps internally.

s1.insights: [ES] Let the grid engine do the math for you.

p1.title: [ES] Layout Sandbox
p1.desc: [ES] Switch between Grid and Flex in real-time.

community.aiSummary: [ES] Shift from float to flex/grid is a major front-end paradigm shift.

t2.title: [ES] Advanced Browser Engines
t2.what: [ES] Understanding how Blink, WebKit, and Gecko handle high-performance animations and heavy computations.
t2.why: [ES] Different engines have different 'Jank' characteristics. Senior engineers know how to avoid the 'Layout Thrash'.
t2.how: [ES] Profile using Chrome DevTools Performance tab and use `requestAnimationFrame`.
t2.ecosystem: [ES] V8, JIT, Compositing, Main Thread, Worker Threads.
t2.realWorld: [ES] Google Search and YouTube optimize for millisecond-level paint times.

useCases.u3: [ES] Handling heavy computation without UI lag

disadvantages.d1: [ES] Micro-optimizations can lead to unreadable code.

t2.interviewTip: [ES] Explain 'Layout Thrashing' as: 'Repeatedly reading and writing to the DOM in a way that forces the browser to recalculate layout multiple times per frame.'
t2.resumeBullet: [ES] Eliminated Layout Thrashing in a real-time analytics dashboard, improving frame-rate from 20 to 60 FPS.

s1.title: [ES] The Stuttering Scroll
s1.description: [ES] A page with 1,000 images stutters and lags significantly during scrolling.
s1.analysis: [ES] The browser is trying to paint too many elements at once, exhausting the 'Compositing Thread'.

t1.name: [ES] will-change: transform
t1.justification: [ES] Signals to the browser to promote the element to its own GPU layer.

tradeOffs.t1: [ES] Excessive layer creation can crash the GPU (Layer Explosion).

s1.insights: [ES] Code for the GPU, not just the CPU.

p1.title: [ES] GPU Layer Sandbox
p1.desc: [ES] Visualize browser 'Layers' and watch how they affect performance.

git.commit: [ES] perf: promoted heavy elements to GPU layers

community.aiSummary: [ES] Rendering performance is the last frontier of front-end engineering.

m3.title: [ES] Modern JavaScript & Interaction
m3.desc: [ES] Mastering the logic and interactive capabilities of the web platform.

t1.title: [ES] Dynamic Interactivity & Event Architecture
t1.what: [ES] Event Architecture studies how user interactions propagate through the Browser Object Model.
t1.why: [ES] Poorly handled listeners cause Memory Leaks and lag.
t1.when: [ES] Crucial forapps with massive grids or real-time dashboards.
t1.how: [ES] Use Event Delegation with a single parent listener.
t1.realWorld: [ES] Infinite Scroll on major social networks uses delegation for speed.
t1.interviewTip: [ES] Event Delegation leverages the 'Bubble' phase for efficiency.
t1.resumeBullet: [ES] Optimized UI performance by 40% using centralized delegation architecture.

s1.title: [ES] The Memory Leak Mystery
s1.description: [ES] Stock ticker crashes browser after an hour due to 4GB RAM usage.
s1.analysis: [ES] Listeners are attached to every new row but never removed.

t1.name: [ES] Chrome DevTools Memory Profiler
t1.justification: [ES] Take Heap Snapshots to find sticky objects.

tradeOffs.t1: [ES] Centralized logic can be more complex.

community.aiSummary: [ES] Event Bubbling is a senior-level architectural tool.

t2.title: [ES] Modern Browser APIs & Storage
t2.what: [ES] Browser APIs (Observer, Storage, Geolocation) provide native desktop-app capabilities.
t2.why: [ES] Avoid hacks like window.onscroll; use native observers for battery-efficient lazy loading.
t2.when: [ES] When building Offline-First or high-end animation apps.
t2.ecosystem: [ES] Intersection Observer, Web Storage, IndexedDB, Web Workers.
t2.realWorld: [ES] Google Maps uses Geolocation for instant positioning.
t2.interviewTip: [ES] Intersection Observer runs in the browser loop, not the JS thread.
t2.resumeBullet: [ES]  aumentó initial page load speed by 60% using native lazy-loading.

s1.title: [ES] The Infinite Jump
s1.description: [ES] Infinite scroll list jumps and users lose their place.
s1.analysis: [ES] Sudden layout shifts due to unreserved space for new items.

t1.name: [ES] Lighthouse CLS
t1.justification: [ES] Measures physical page movement during loading.

p1.title: [ES] Lazy Loader Sandbox
p1.desc: [ES] Watch images load as they enter the viewport.

community.aiSummary: [ES] Browser APIs bridge the gap between document coding and platform engineering.

m4.title: [ES] Full-Stack Integration & PWAs
m4.desc: [ES] Connecting to servers and building offline-ready applications.

t1.title: [ES] Asynchronous Engineering & Network Patterns
t1.what: [ES] Promises and Async/Await handle long-running operations like API calls.
t1.why: [ES] JavaScript is single-threaded; async keeps the UI active during fetches.
t1.when: [ES] Talking to databases or external APIs.
t1.ecosystem: [ES] Promises, Async/Await, Fetch API, Event Loop.
t1.realWorld: [ES] Checking bank balances happens while UI stays responsive.
t1.interviewTip: [ES] Event Loop picks from Callback Queue to Call Stack when empty.
t1.resumeBullet: [ES] Reduced fetch latency by 45% using parallel promise resolution.

s1.title: [ES] The Race Condition
s1.description: [ES] User search box shows outdated results as requests finish out of order.

t1.name: [ES] AbortController
t1.justification: [ES] Cancels old requests when new ones start.

s1.insights: [ES] Dispatch order is not arrival order.

community.aiSummary: [ES] Event Loop is the engine; understand it to avoid UI lag.

t2.title: [ES] Progressive Web Apps & Offline Architecture
t2.what: [ES] PWAs use Service Workers to cache files for offline availability.
t2.why: [ES] Mobile internet is spotty; PWAs work in tunnels or planes.
t2.when: [ES] Mobile-first experiences or field worker tools.
t2.ecosystem: [ES] Service Workers, Manifest, Cache Storage, Offline-First.

useCases.u2: [ES] Syncing work in zones without internet

t2.interviewTip: [ES] PWAs are just standard apps following the PWA checklist.
t2.resumeBullet: [ES] Engineered an offline-first PWA for zero-connectivity environments.

s1.title: [ES] The Cache Invalidation Trap
s1.description: [ES] Users see old versions because service workers stay active.
s1.analysis: [ES] Sticky service workers don't update until tabs are closed.

kpis.k1: [ES] Reduce version mismatch tickets to zero.

s1.insights: [ES] Cache invalidation is a hard problem.

capstone.title: [ES] The 'Omni-Channel' E-Commerce Engine
capstone.description: [ES] Build a high-performance e-commerce dashboard that works offline, lazy-loads 1,000+ products, and uses a BEM design system.

step1.title: [ES] Atomic Design System
step1.objective: [ES] Build core components using BEM and CSS Variables.

step2.title: [ES] Grid Dashboard Layout
step2.objective: [ES] Architect app shell using CSS Grid.

step4.title: [ES] The Offline Edge
step4.objective: [ES] Implement Service Worker for catalog caching.

step5.title: [ES] Performance Optimization
step5.objective: [ES] Audit with Lighthouse and implement native lazy-loading.

hceBack101.title: [ES] High-Code Backend Engineering 101
hceBack101.prerequisites: [ES] Advanced JavaScript, Basic SQL, Networking Basics
hceBack101.targetAudience: [ES] Frontend Developers moving to Fullstack, Aspiring System Architects
learningOutcomes: [ES] Master ACID transactions and NoSQL scaling
[ES] Build Event-Driven architectures with Message Queues

m1.title: [ES] The Foundation: Protocols & Networking
m1.description: [ES] Mastering the low-level communication that powers the modern web.
outcomes: [ES] Deep dive into OSI Model & TCP/UDP
[ES] Configure Nginx for Load Balancing & Caching

t1.title: [ES] OSI Model, TCP/IP & DNS
t1.what: [ES] The architectural framework for how data moves across the internet.
t1.why: [ES] Without understanding packets and frames, you cannot debug high-latency systems or secure internal networks.
t1.when: [ES] Mandatory for every backend developer and SRE.
t1.how: [ES] Using tools like Wireshark to inspect packets and dig/nslookup for DNS resolution.
t1.realWorld: [ES] Cloudflare uses these protocols at the edge to mitigate DDoS attacks and speed up world-wide content delivery.
t1.interviewTip: [ES] Be able to explain the 3-way handshake in detail.
t1.resumeBullet: [ES] Optimized network packet flow, reducing latency by 15% through protocol tuning.

s1.title: [ES] Network Debugging for E-commerce
s1.scenarioDescription: [ES] A checkout service is timing out during high traffic. Is it DNS, TCP congestion, or application logic?
s1.problemAnalysis: [ES] Bottleneck in the TCP handshake during peak load.
s1.tool1Justification: [ES] Using Wireshark to pinpoint the exact level where packets are dropping.
s1.tradeOff1: [ES] Latency vs Accuracy during packet inspection.
s1.keyInsights: [ES] TCP Congestion control is a common silent performance killer.

t2.title: [ES] Load Balancing & Reverse Proxies
t2.what: [ES] Distributing traffic across multiple servers to ensure high availability.
t2.why: [ES] One server will eventually fail. Load balancing prevents single points of failure.
t2.when: [ES] Whenever scaling beyond a single instance.
t2.how: [ES] Using Nginx, HAProxy, or Cloud Load Balancers (AWS ALB).
t2.realWorld: [ES] Netflix uses Envoy proxy to manage millions of concurrent microservice calls.
t2.interviewTip: [ES] Understand Round Robin vs Least Connections algorithms.
t2.resumeBullet: [ES] Implemented Nginx reverse proxy, improving system throughput by 40%.

s1.title: [ES] Global Scaling for Video Streaming
s1.scenarioDescription: [ES] Design a load balancing layer that routes users to the nearest healthy datacenter.
s1.problemAnalysis: [ES] Geographic latency is causing buffering for international users.
s1.tool1Justification: [ES] Nginx for its robust L7 routing and caching capabilities.
s1.tradeOff1: [ES] Complexity of multi-region vs cost of single region.
s1.keyInsights: [ES] Health checks are the core of any reliable load balancer.

t3.title: [ES] Distributed Systems & CAP Theorem
t3.what: [ES] The trade-offs between Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.
t3.why: [ES] In a distributed world, you cannot have all three. You must choose based on business needs.
t3.when: [ES] Building any system with more than one database node.
t3.how: [ES] By selecting the right database (SQL vs NoSQL) and consensus algorithms.
t3.realWorld: [ES] Google Spanner uses TrueTime to provide global consistency at the cost of slight latency.
t3.interviewTip: [ES] Explain how a bank might prioritize C over A during a network split.
t3.resumeBullet: [ES] Redesigned distributed architecture, ensuring 99.99% availability during network splits.

s1.title: [ES] Consistent Hashing for Caching
s1.scenarioDescription: [ES] Implement a caching layer that minimizes re-shuffling when nodes are added or removed.
s1.problemAnalysis: [ES] Standard modulo hashing causes 99% cache miss on node failure.
s1.tool1Justification: [ES] Consistent Hashing Algorithm for minimal partition movement.
s1.keyInsights: [ES] Consistent hashing is fundamental for scalable stateful systems.

t1.title: [ES] Idempotent API Design
t1.what: [ES] Ensuring repeated requests have the same effect as a single request.
t1.why: [ES] Prevents double charging or duplicate record creation during retries.
t1.how: [ES] Using unique Idempotency-Keys and Redis locks.
t1.realWorld: [ES] Stripe uses Idempotency-Keys for every single transaction.
t1.interviewTip: [ES] Explain why POST is not idempotent by default, but PUT is.
t1.resumeBullet: [ES] Implemented enterprise-grade idempotency, eliminating 100% of duplicate transactions.

s1.title: [ES] Designing the Idempotency Middleware
s1.scenarioDescription: [ES] Build a robust middleware interceptor that catches every incoming transaction request.
s1.problemAnalysis: [ES] Parallel race conditions in bank transfers.
s1.keyInsights: [ES] Atomic operations are your only defense against concurrency.
s1.summary: [ES] Focus on Redis SETNX for atomic locks.
s1.author: [ES] James L.

community.aiSummary: [ES] Use atomic locks, do not rely on database application-level IF statements.

s2.summary: [ES] Nginx caching can help but idempotency is app-level.
s2.author: [ES] Sarah A.

git.commitMessage: [ES] feat: Add redis SETNX idempotency middleware

t2.title: [ES] REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC
t2.what: [ES] Comparing different communication protocols and styles.
t2.why: [ES] Different constraints require different tools for optimization.
t2.when: [ES] When starting a new service or migrating legacy ones.
t2.how: [ES] Defining contracts via OpenAPI, Protobuf, or GraphQL Schemas.
t2.realWorld: [ES] Netflix uses gRPC for internal and GraphQL for external APIs.
t2.interviewTip: [ES] Know when to choose gRPC (performance) over REST (flexibility).
t2.resumeBullet: [ES] Reduced internal latency by 70% by migrating microservices to gRPC.

s1.title: [ES] Migrating to gRPC
s1.scenarioDescription: [ES] Migrate a high-traffic REST service to gRPC to reduce CPU overhead and latency.
s1.problemAnalysis: [ES] JSON parsing is consuming 40% of service CPU.
s1.keyInsights: [ES] Contracts prevent breaking changes in polyglot systems.
s1.summary: [ES] Protobuf is significantly faster than JSON parsing.
s1.author: [ES] Mike K.

f1.code: [ES] type User { id: ID! name: String! }

community.aiSummary: [ES] gRPC for internal, REST/GraphQL for external.

s1.summary: [ES] gRPC reduced our inter-service latency drastically.
s1.author: [ES] Mike K.

hceDevops101.title: [ES] HCE DevOps 101: Linux, Docker & CI/CD Foundations
hceDevops101.description: [ES] Master the tools that power modern product teams: Linux fundamentals, Docker containerization, and automated CI/CD pipelines from scratch.
hceDevops101.targetAudience: [ES] Junior developers, fresh graduates, and anyone looking to break into DevOps & cloud engineering.

outcomes.o3: [ES] Automate testing and deployments with GitHub Actions
outcomes.o4: [ES] Write Python/Bash scripts to automate ops tasks
outcomes.o5: [ES] Understand and use Git branching strategies for teams

m1.title: [ES] Linux Fundamentals & Shell Mastery
m1.description: [ES] Navigate, control, and automate Linux systems from the terminal.

outcomes.o1: [ES] Master file system navigation and permissions
outcomes.o3: [ES] Manage processes, users and system services

m2.title: [ES] Networking, SSH & Security Basics
m2.description: [ES] Secure your infrastructure by mastering SSH, firewall rules, and network diagnostics.

outcomes.o1: [ES] Set up secure SSH key-based authentication
outcomes.o3: [ES] Diagnose network issues with standard CLI tools

m3.title: [ES] Docker & Container Architecture
m3.description: [ES] Package applications into reproducible, secure containers ready for any environment.

outcomes.o2: [ES] Orchestrate multi-service apps with Docker Compose

m4.title: [ES] Python & Bash Scripting for Automation
m4.description: [ES] Automate repetitive operations, system checks, and data processing tasks.

outcomes.o1: [ES] Write Python scripts to automate DevOps tasks
outcomes.o3: [ES] Schedule and monitor automated jobs with cron

m5.title: [ES] Git Branching Strategies & CI/CD Pipelines
m5.description: [ES] Implement professional Git workflows and automate your entire build-test-deploy cycle.

outcomes.o1: [ES] Implement Git Flow and trunk-based development
outcomes.o2: [ES] Build CI/CD pipelines that auto-test and deploy

t1.title: [ES] Linux File System, Permissions & Process Management
t1.what: [ES] The Linux filesystem hierarchy and its permission model (owner/group/others, read/write/execute).
t1.why: [ES] Every cloud server runs Linux. Without this knowledge, you cannot secure, debug, or operate any production system.
t1.when: [ES] Whenever you provision a server, deploy to cloud VMs, or debug production incidents.
t1.how: [ES] Using chmod, chown, ps, kill, and systemctl commands to control the system.
t1.ecosystem: [ES] Ubuntu, Debian, CentOS, AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine, systemd.
t1.realWorld: [ES] Netflix engineers use Linux process management to ensure their Java processes never exceed memory thresholds on EC2 instances.

advantages.ad1: [ES] Universal: 90%+ of cloud infrastructure runs Linux
advantages.ad2: [ES] Granular permission model prevents security breaches

disadvantages.da1: [ES] Steep initial learning curve for GUI-trained developers
disadvantages.da2: [ES] Misconfigured permissions can lock out entire teams

t1.interviewTip: [ES] Be able to explain chmod 600, 755, and 777 and the security implications of each.
t1.resumeBullet: [ES] Hardened Linux servers using principle of least privilege, eliminating unauthorized access incidents.

b1.title: [ES] Linux File System & Permissions Deep Dive

s1.title: [ES] Securing a Rogue Process on a Production API Server
s1.description: [ES] An unknown process is consuming 100% CPU on your production server. Identify it, kill it, and prevent it from restarting.
s1.analysis: [ES] Without process management skills, a single misbehaving process can bring down an entire service for all users.

tools.j1: [ES] chown/usermod to isolate the process user, and systemctl to remove its autostart entry.

tradeOffs.t1: [ES] Killing immediately vs. capturing logs for post-mortem analysis.

kpis.k1: [ES] CPU usage returns to < 10% within 120 seconds.

s1.insights: [ES] Always run services under dedicated, least-privilege system users, never as root.

p1.title: [ES] Linux File Permissions Workshop
p1.description: [ES] Practice chmod symbolic and octal modes to set precise access controls on sensitive files.

git.commit: [ES] security: Isolate API service under dedicated non-root user
git.snippetCode:
[ES] #!/bin/bash
# Harden: run as non-root
useradd -r apiuser
chown -R apiuser /var/app

community.summary: [ES] The #1 security advice from senior engineers: never run anything as root in production. Always use dedicated service accounts with minimal permissions.

t3.title: [ES] Docker: Containerizing Applications with Multi-Stage Builds
t3.what: [ES] Docker packages your application and all its dependencies into a portable, isolated unit called a container.
t3.why: [ES] Eliminates 'it works on my machine' forever. Containers are identical across dev, staging, and production environments.
t3.when: [ES] For every application you deploy to the cloud or share across a team.
t3.how: [ES] Writing Dockerfiles, building images, and running containers with docker run and docker compose.
t3.ecosystem: [ES] Docker Hub, GitHub Container Registry, AWS ECR, Google Artifact Registry, BuildKit.
t3.realWorld: [ES] Airbnb containerized 1,000+ microservices with Docker to achieve consistent deployments across data centers worldwide.

useCases.uc1: [ES] Packaging Node.js/Python/Go apps for cloud deployment
useCases.uc2: [ES] Running local development environments identically across teams
useCases.uc3: [ES] Creating minimal, secure production images with multi-stage builds

advantages.ad2: [ES] Multi-stage builds slash image size by 80%+
advantages.ad3: [ES] Instant container spin-up vs. minutes for VMs

disadvantages.da1: [ES] Container security requires expertise (secret management, image scanning)
disadvantages.da2: [ES] Not ideal for stateful applications without proper persistent volume management

t3.interviewTip: [ES] Explain the difference between COPY and ADD in a Dockerfile, and why multi-stage builds reduce attack surface.
t3.resumeBullet: [ES] Reduced production Docker image sizes by 75% using multi-stage builds, cutting deployment times by 40%.

s1.title: [ES] Shrink a 2GB Production Docker Image to Under 200MB
s1.description: [ES] Your team's Docker image takes 8 minutes to push/pull during deployments, slowing down releases.
s1.analysis: [ES] Large images contain build tools, dev dependencies, and source maps — none of which should exist in production.

tools.j1: [ES] BuildKit's cache mounts and multi-stage COPY allow surgical control over what ends up in the production layer.

tradeOffs.t1: [ES] Build complexity increases slightly, but deployment speed and security improve drastically.

nfr.n1: [ES] Image size < 200MB. Pull time < 45 seconds on standard network.

kpis.k1: [ES] Deployment pipeline duration reduced from 12 to 4 minutes.

s1.insights: [ES] Use .dockerignore aggressively. Your node_modules, .git folder, and tests should NEVER be in a production image.

p1.title: [ES] Multi-Stage Dockerfile Builder
p1.description: [ES] Write a two-stage Dockerfile that compiles a Node.js app and copies only the artifact to an Alpine runtime image.

git.commit: [ES] perf: Implement multi-stage Docker build, reducing image from 2.1GB to 185MB

f1.content:
[ES] FROM node:20-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]

git.snippetCode:
[ES] # Only the final stage ends up in the production image
FROM node:20-alpine AS build
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist

community.summary: [ES] Senior engineers consistently emphasize: build for production, not development. Multi-stage builds and .dockerignore files are non-negotiable in any professional CI/CD pipeline.

t5.title: [ES] Git Branching Strategies & GitHub Actions CI/CD
t5.what: [ES] Structured Git workflows (Git Flow, Trunk-Based) and automated pipelines that test, build, and deploy code on every push.
t5.why: [ES] Manual deployments cause 80% of outages. CI/CD eliminates human error and ensures only tested code reaches production.
t5.when: [ES] Immediately on any team project with more than one developer or any project with a staging/production environment.
t5.how: [ES] Using Git branches for feature isolation and YAML-based GitHub Actions workflows to automate the entire software lifecycle.
t5.ecosystem: [ES] GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI, ArgoCD, SonarQube.
t5.realWorld: [ES] Amazon deploys to production an average of every 11.6 seconds. This is only possible with fully automated CI/CD pipelines.

useCases.uc1: [ES] Automatically running tests on every pull request
useCases.uc2: [ES] Deploying to staging on merge to main, production on tag
useCases.uc3: [ES] Scanning for secrets and vulnerabilities before code ships

advantages.ad1: [ES] Automated testing catches regressions before production
advantages.ad2: [ES] Consistent, repeatable deployments across all environments
advantages.ad3: [ES] Audit trail of every deployment with rollback capability

disadvantages.da1: [ES] Initial pipeline setup requires significant time investment
disadvantages.da2: [ES] Complex pipelines become fragile if not properly maintained

t5.interviewTip: [ES] Be able to explain the difference between continuous delivery (human approval gate) and continuous deployment (fully automated).
t5.resumeBullet: [ES] Designed and implemented a GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline reducing release cycle from weekly to multiple times per day.

s1.title: [ES] A Secret Credential Was Committed to the Public GitHub Repo
s1.description: [ES] A developer accidentally committed an AWS API key to a public repository. It has been live for 3 hours and costs are spiking.
s1.analysis: [ES] Once a secret is committed to Git history, it persists even after deletion from HEAD. The attacker already likely scraped it.

tools.j1: [ES] TruffleHog scans Git history for high-entropy strings to detect secrets committed at any point in history.

tradeOffs.t1: [ES] Rewriting Git history (force push) is disruptive to team but mandatory after a credential leak.

nfr.n1: [ES] MTTR (Mean Time to Remediation) < 15 minutes for leaked credential.

kpis.k1: [ES] Zero recurrence of secret leaks after implementing pre-commit hooks.

s1.insights: [ES] Secrets should NEVER live in code. Use a secret manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) from day one.

p1.title: [ES] Build Your First CI Pipeline
p1.description: [ES] Write a GitHub Actions workflow that installs dependencies, runs tests, and reports results on every push to main.

git.commit: [ES] ci: Add GitHub Actions pipeline with test, build, and security scan stages

f1.content:
[ES] name: CI
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm install && npm test

git.snippetCode:
[ES] # Full CI/CD Pipeline
name: CI
on: [push]
jobs:
  pipeline:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm test
      - run: npm run build

community.summary: [ES] The single highest-ROI engineering investment for most teams is building a reliable CI pipeline. It is universally considered non-negotiable for professional software development.

capstone.title: [ES] Operation: Production-Ready Cloud App
capstone.description: [ES] Deploy a full-stack application to a cloud VM using Docker, secure it with HTTPS, and automate its deployment via GitHub Actions.

p1.title: [ES] Provision & Harden a Cloud VM
p1.description: [ES] Create an Ubuntu server on AWS/GCP, configure SSH keys, set up a non-root user, and configure UFW firewall rules.

p2.title: [ES] Containerize the Application
p2.description: [ES] Write a multi-stage Dockerfile and docker-compose.yml for the web app and its database.

p3.title: [ES] Configure Nginx & SSL
p3.description: [ES] Set up Nginx as a reverse proxy and obtain a free Let's Encrypt SSL certificate.

p4.title: [ES] Build the CI/CD Pipeline
p4.description: [ES] Create a GitHub Actions workflow that automatically builds, tests, and SSHs into the server to deploy the new container on every push to main.

hceDevops201.title: [ES] HCE DevOps 201: Kubernetes, Terraform & SRE Mastery
hceDevops201.description: [ES] Operate planet-scale infrastructure. Master Terraform IaC, Kubernetes orchestration, Prometheus/Grafana observability, and Site Reliability Engineering principles.
hceDevops201.targetAudience: [ES] Mid-to-senior DevOps engineers, SREs, and platform engineers with solid Docker and CI/CD experience.

outcomes.o1: [ES] Provision multi-region cloud infrastructure with Terraform
outcomes.o2: [ES] Deploy and operate production-grade Kubernetes clusters
outcomes.o3: [ES] Build world-class observability stacks with Prometheus & Grafana
outcomes.o4: [ES] Define and enforce SLOs/SLIs/Error Budgets as an SRE

m1.title: [ES] Terraform & Infrastructure as Code
m1.description: [ES] Declare, version-control, and automate the provisioning of entire cloud environments.

outcomes.o2: [ES] Manage remote state safely with S3/GCS backends
outcomes.o3: [ES] Handle infrastructure drift and state conflicts

m2.title: [ES] Kubernetes: Pods, Deployments & Advanced Scheduling
m2.description: [ES] Deploy, autoscale, and operate containerized applications on enterprise Kubernetes clusters.

outcomes.o2: [ES] Configure HPA and VPA for intelligent autoscaling
outcomes.o3: [ES] Manage cluster resources with namespaces and quotas

m3.title: [ES] SRE: Observability, SLOs & Incident Management
m3.description: [ES] Build the monitoring, alerting, and incident management systems that ensure reliability.

outcomes.o2: [ES] Define actionable SLIs, SLOs, and Error Budgets

m4.title: [ES] GitOps & ArgoCD
m4.description: [ES] Make your Git repository the single source of truth for all cluster state.

outcomes.o2: [ES] Implement automated drift detection and reconciliation
outcomes.o3: [ES] Build blue-green and canary deployments with Argo Rollouts

m5.title: [ES] Security, Secrets & Zero-Trust Networking
m5.description: [ES] Harden your Kubernetes cluster with RBAC, network policies, and enterprise secret management.

outcomes.o1: [ES] Implement Kubernetes RBAC and Pod Security Standards
outcomes.o2: [ES] Manage secrets with HashiCorp Vault and External Secrets Operator
outcomes.o3: [ES] Enforce zero-trust networking with Calico network policies

t1.title: [ES] Terraform: Infrastructure as Code from Zero to Production
t1.what: [ES] Terraform allows you to define cloud infrastructure (VMs, databases, networks) in declarative code that can be versioned, reviewed, and automated.
t1.why: [ES] Manual cloud console clicks are not reproducible, auditable, or teamwork-friendly. IaC is the standard in every mature engineering organization.
t1.when: [ES] Any time you provision cloud resources — from a single EC2 instance to a multi-region, multi-cloud architecture.
t1.how: [ES] Writing .tf files with providers, resources, and outputs. Running terraform plan, apply, and destroy to manage the lifecycle.
t1.ecosystem: [ES] Terraform, Terragrunt, Pulumi, AWS CDK, OpenTofu, Atlantis, Spacelift.
t1.realWorld: [ES] Stripe manages tens of thousands of cloud resources across multiple providers with Terraform, ensuring every change is peer-reviewed before applying.

useCases.uc1: [ES] Spinning up identical staging and production environments

advantages.ad1: [ES] Full infrastructure reproducibility eliminates environment drift
advantages.ad2: [ES] Peer-reviewed infrastructure changes via pull requests
advantages.ad3: [ES] One command to destroy and rebuild entire environments

disadvantages.da1: [ES] State file management is complex at scale and requires locking
disadvantages.da2: [ES] Steep learning curve for teams unfamiliar with declarative paradigms

t1.interviewTip: [ES] Explain the difference between terraform plan and apply, and why remote state with locking is mandatory in team environments.
t1.resumeBullet: [ES] Migrated 200+ manual cloud resources to Terraform IaC, reducing provisioning time from 3 days to 15 minutes.

s1.title: [ES] Recovering from a Terraform State Corruption After a Team Conflict
s1.description: [ES] Two engineers applied changes simultaneously without state locking, causing conflicting state entries and failed applies.
s1.analysis: [ES] Without remote state locking (e.g., DynamoDB for S3 backends), race conditions corrupt the state file, making Terraform unusable until manually resolved.

tools.j1: [ES] terraform import allows re-importing manually created resources back into state, while terraform state rm removes orphaned references.

tradeOffs.t1: [ES] Manual state surgery is risky and requires team coordination. Investing 30 mins in proper remote state setup saves hours of future incidents.

nfr.n1: [ES] State backend must have 100% consistency guarantees (strong read-after-write, atomic locks).

kpis.k1: [ES] Zero state corruption incidents after enabling DynamoDB locking.

s1.insights: [ES] Remote state with locking is non-negotiable on any team. Use S3 + DynamoDB (AWS) or GCS (GCP) from day one, not later.

p1.title: [ES] Provision Your First Cloud Resource with Terraform
p1.description: [ES] Write a Terraform config to provision an EC2 instance and an S3 bucket, then output the public IP.

git.commit: [ES] infra: Add Terraform module for production VPC and EC2 autoscaling group

community.summary: [ES] The Terraform community is united on one principle: never use local state in production. Remote backends with locking prevent data loss and team conflicts universally.

t2.title: [ES] Kubernetes: Deployments, Services & Horizontal Pod Autoscaling
t2.what: [ES] Kubernetes (K8s) is the industry-standard container orchestration platform for deploying, scaling, and managing containerized applications.
t2.why: [ES] Without orchestration, you manually manage server capacity, deployments, and failures. Kubernetes automates all of this at scale.
t2.when: [ES] When your application needs high availability, autoscaling, or multi-service deployment on more than one server.
t2.how: [ES] Declaring Kubernetes manifests (Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, HPAs) in YAML and applying them with kubectl.
t2.ecosystem: [ES] kubectl, Helm, Kustomize, ArgoCD, GKE, EKS, AKS, k3s.
t2.realWorld: [ES] Google runs billions of containers per week internally on Borg (Kubernetes' predecessor). Every major tech company now runs Kubernetes in production.

useCases.uc1: [ES] Auto-scaling to handle traffic spikes during product launches
useCases.uc3: [ES] Resource management ensuring no single pod starves others

advantages.ad2: [ES] Declarative: desired state is always enforced
advantages.ad3: [ES] Rich ecosystem of CNCF tools integrates natively

disadvantages.da1: [ES] Significant operational complexity — not appropriate for simple apps
disadvantages.da2: [ES] Networking model is complex and requires deep understanding to debug

t2.interviewTip: [ES] Explain the difference between a Pod and a Deployment, and what HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) monitors to scale.
t2.resumeBullet: [ES] Deployed and operated production Kubernetes workloads serving 50K+ concurrent users with 99.95% uptime.

s1.title: [ES] Diagnosing an OOMKilled Pod Crash Loop in Production
s1.description: [ES] A critical payments microservice is stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff. kubectl describe shows OOMKilled reason.
s1.analysis: [ES] The container's memory limit in its Kubernetes resource spec is too low for peak-traffic request volumes, causing the OOM killer to terminate it.

tools.j1: [ES] Resource Quotas and LimitRanges establish memory floors and ceilings. VPA (Vertical Pod Autoscaler) can then recommend optimal resource values based on actual usage.

tradeOffs.t1: [ES] Setting limits too high wastes cluster resources. Too low causes OOMKills. Always profile production workloads to find the correct range.

nfr.n1: [ES] P99 latency < 200ms. Zero OOMKill events under normal load.

kpis.k1: [ES] Pod restarts per hour = 0 after tuning resource limits.

s1.insights: [ES] Always set both requests AND limits. Requests guarantee scheduling, limits prevent noisy neighbor problems.

p1.title: [ES] Deploy a 3-Replica App & Autoscale It
p1.description: [ES] Write a Deployment and HPA manifest, then simulate CPU load to watch Kubernetes scale your pods automatically.

git.commit: [ES] feat: Add HPA for payments service with CPU and memory scale targets

f1.content:
[ES] apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: payments-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: payments
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70

git.snippetCode:
[ES] # HPA: Scale when CPU exceeds 70%
scaleTargetRef:
  name: payments
minReplicas: 2
maxReplicas: 20
metrics:
- type: Resource
  resource:
    name: cpu
    target:
      averageUtilization: 70

community.summary: [ES] Senior SREs emphasize: start with sane resource limits and let VPA guide you to production-accurate values. Most Kubernetes incidents are caused by misconfigured resource limits, not application bugs.

t3.title: [ES] SRE: SLIs, SLOs, Error Budgets & Prometheus Alerting
t3.what: [ES] Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) is the practice of applying software engineering rigor to operations — measuring, automating, and improving reliability systematically.
t3.why: [ES] Without SLOs and error budgets, reliability is a feeling, not a number. SRE turns reliability into a measurable, actionable product requirement.
t3.when: [ES] In any service that has a user-facing API or user experience that must meet defined availability guarantees.
t3.how: [ES] Defining SLIs (metrics), SLOs (targets), and Error Budgets (tolerance), then alerting on burn rate with Prometheus and Grafana.
t3.ecosystem: [ES] Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry, PagerDuty, Alertmanager, Google SRE Book.
t3.realWorld: [ES] Google's SRE teams use Error Budgets to balance reliability vs. feature velocity. If the error budget is exhausted, no new features ship until reliability is restored.

useCases.uc1: [ES] Setting formal uptime SLAs for enterprise customers
useCases.uc2: [ES] Building automated burn-rate alerts before SLO breach
useCases.uc3: [ES] Using error budgets to make data-driven release decisions

advantages.ad1: [ES] Transforms reliability from intuition to measurable engineering
advantages.ad2: [ES] Error budgets create objective, team-wide reliability contracts
advantages.ad3: [ES] Burn-rate alerting catches problems before customers notice

disadvantages.da1: [ES] Requires buy-in from product, engineering, and leadership
disadvantages.da2: [ES] Complex PromQL queries and alerting rules require expertise to tune correctly

t3.interviewTip: [ES] Be able to calculate an error budget: if SLO is 99.9%, the error budget for 30 days is 0.1% of 43,200 minutes = 43.2 minutes of allowed downtime.
t3.resumeBullet: [ES] Implemented SLI/SLO framework and Prometheus burn-rate alerting, reducing MTTD (Mean Time to Detect) from 30 min to 90 seconds.

m1.title: [ES] Site Reliability Engineering: Measuring and Managing Reliability

s1.title: [ES] Your SLO Is Breaching — Diagnose the Error Budget Burn
s1.description: [ES] Grafana alerts show the 30-day error budget for the checkout API will be exhausted in 4 hours at the current burn rate, but no obvious errors appear in logs.
s1.analysis: [ES] High burn rate can indicate latency degradation (slow responses that still return 200 OK) rather than outright errors — a silent failure mode that standard error-rate alerts miss.

tools.j1: [ES] Prometheus multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting as defined in the Google SRE Workbook catches both short-term spikes and slow, sustained degradation.

tradeOffs.t1: [ES] Tight SLOs (99.99%) leave almost no room for releases. Loose SLOs (99%) are too permissive. Choose based on customer contracts and incident impact.

nfr.n1: [ES] P99 latency < 500ms. Error rate < 0.1% of all requests over 30 days.

kpis.k1: [ES] MTTD < 2 minutes. Error budget consumption < 50% per month.

s1.insights: [ES] Alerting on error rate alone is insufficient. Always alert on error budget BURN RATE using both short (1h) and long (6h) windows simultaneously.

p1.title: [ES] Write PromQL SLO Queries
p1.description: [ES] Use PromQL to calculate the ratio of good requests to total requests and express it as a 28-day rolling SLO percentage.

git.commit: [ES] sre: Add multi-window burn-rate alerting rules for checkout SLO

f1.content:
[ES] groups:
- name: slo.rules
  rules:
  - alert: CheckoutHighBurnRate
    expr: |
      (
        sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="checkout",code!~"5.."}[5m]))
        /
        sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="checkout"}[5m]))
      ) < 0.999
    for: 2m
    labels:
      severity: page

git.snippetCode:
[ES] # Alert when error rate exceeds SLO threshold
expr: |
  sum(rate(http_requests_total{code!~"5.."}[5m]))
  / sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])) < 0.999

community.summary: [ES] The SRE community agrees: the most valuable insight from Google's SRE book is the Error Budget concept. It creates a shared language between product (who wants features) and ops (who wants reliability). Both sides win when the budget is managed well.

capstone.title: [ES] Operation: Planet-Scale Platform
capstone.description: [ES] Provision a production-grade, multi-environment Kubernetes platform on a major cloud provider using Terraform, deploy a microservice with ArgoCD, and build a full observability stack.

p1.title: [ES] Provision the Kubernetes Cluster with Terraform
p1.description: [ES] Write Terraform modules to create a GKE/EKS cluster with separate node pools for system and application workloads.

p2.title: [ES] Deploy the Application with ArgoCD
p2.description: [ES] Connect ArgoCD to a Git repository and configure automated sync to deploy the microservice Helm chart on every commit.

p3.title: [ES] Build the Observability Stack
p3.description: [ES] Deploy Prometheus and Grafana using the kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart and create custom dashboards for your service's SLIs.

p4.title: [ES] Define and Alert on SLOs
p4.description: [ES] Write Prometheus alerting rules based on multi-window burn rate to page on-call when the error budget is being consumed too fast.
